policy_statement: Public Health and Social Services: improve community health indicators by adopting Health-in-all-policies, expanding healthcare capacity and access, completing UNLV School of Medicine build-out, and developing local Health and Wellness Centers.
mechanism_description: Strengthening healthcare and wellness capacity improves community health resilience and adaptive capacity to climate-related stresses (e.g., heat, smoke, extreme events).
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard exposure → strengthened public health/healthcare capacity → improved preparedness/response → ↓ health impacts (↓ vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Governments and health institutions expand and improve healthcare delivery and public health functions (e.g., more hospital/ICU capacity, better access in underserved areas, health-in-all-policies coordination, workforce/training, local wellness centers, stronger interagency partnerships).  
2) Mediating process: Improved surveillance, prevention, risk communication, continuity of operations, triage/treatment surge capacity, and equitable access enable faster and more effective response to heat illness, respiratory impacts from smoke/dust, injuries from extreme events, and post-disaster disease/mental health burdens.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced climate-attributable health damages (lower morbidity/mortality), improved recovery, and higher institutional adaptive capacity across climate extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced mortality and morbidity during climate hazards (especially extreme heat and smoke) due to increased healthcare/public health capacity and access.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate hazards increase health risk; strengthening health systems reduces vulnerability and impacts through better preparedness, response, and access.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The direct intended and typical effect is managing climate-related health burdens (adaptation), not reducing GHG emissions (mitigation) or reducing material/energy inputs (resource efficiency) or relying on ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits may occur if facilities are upgraded, but they are not unavoidable consequences of health system strengthening.  
4) Edge cases applied: Some health investments could be paired with low-carbon facility standards; however, the mechanism as defined does not require emissions reductions, so it remains Adaptation without inherent secondary categories.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that strengthens public health/healthcare capacity in general increases adaptive capacity to climate-related stresses; thus Adaptation consistently applies.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “strengthening” explicitly includes decarbonizing healthcare operations (e.g., renewable backup power, efficiency retrofits) the policy could warrant a separate mitigation/resource-efficiency mechanism; absent that explicit linkage, classify under Adaptation only.
additional_secondary_evidence: Public health (heat/air quality resilience): Las Vegas faces extreme heat and dust storms, and expanding healthcare/ICU capacity, improving access in underserved areas, and developing local Health and Wellness Centers directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because it increases prevention and treatment capacity for heat illness and respiratory impacts, lowering morbidity/mortality during heat waves and poor-air-quality events.
key_indicators: “improve community health indicators”; “Adopting Health-in-all-policies”; “Increasing hospital and ICU capacity”; “health care access in medically underserved areas”; “Completing the build-out of the UNLV School of Medicine”; “Developing local Health and Wellness Centers”
co_benefits: Improved general population health and equity; workforce development/medical training capacity; better routine healthcare access (non-climate health outcomes)
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type could also be coded as a mixed package (planning-strategy for “Health-in-all-policies” plus infrastructure-investment for capacity build-out); classified as infrastructure-investment because the dominant levers are expanding physical/service capacity (hospital/ICU, centers, build-out).
